metadata.subject: Social Studies
metadata.description: Urban Studies 12 examines urbanization as a global phenomenon and the forces that shape how cities develop, function, and change. Students investigate the history of urban settlement, the functions of local government, urban planning principles, and contemporary challenges facing cities — from housing affordability to climate resilience. The course develops civic literacy and an understanding of how planning decisions affect quality of life.
metadata.general_description: An examination of urbanization as a global force, exploring how cities form, how they are governed, how planning decisions are made, and how contemporary cities can become more livable, equitable, and sustainable.

big_ideas.statement: Urbanization is a critical force that shapes both human life and the planet.
big_ideas.elaboration: More than half the world's population now lives in cities, and urbanization is reshaping economies, environments, cultures, and political systems at a global scale.

big_ideas.statement: The historical development of cities has been shaped by geographic, economic, political, and social factors.
big_ideas.elaboration: Cities didn't just happen — they were built by specific historical forces, from ancient river valley civilizations to the industrial boom towns of the 19th century.

big_ideas.statement: Decision making in urban and regional planning requires balancing political, economic, social, and environmental factors.
big_ideas.elaboration: Every planning decision involves trade-offs between growth and sustainability, efficiency and equity, individual rights and collective needs.

big_ideas.statement: Urban planning decisions and other government policies can dramatically affect the overall quality of life in cities.
big_ideas.elaboration: Zoning, transit, housing policy, and public space design all shape whether cities are livable, equitable, and sustainable — or not.

categories.name: Use Social Studies inquiry processes and skills
categories.raw: Draw conclusions, assess and defend positions, demonstrate leadership, identify problems, evaluate data, interpret maps/graphs/tables, present data in various forms, cite sources, construct maps and graphs.

competencies.raw: Use Social Studies inquiry processes to investigate urban issues and communicate findings.
i_can_statements: I can research an urban planning issue and present a well-structured recommendation.
I can interpret urban planning maps, zoning maps, and demographic data.
I can construct a map or data visualization to communicate an urban finding.
I can lead or participate constructively in a planning simulation or debate.

categories.name: Significance
categories.raw: Assess and compare the significance of past and present factors that influence urbanization.

competencies.raw: Assess and compare the significance of factors that influence urbanization.
i_can_statements: I can evaluate which factors (economic, geographic, political) most shaped the development of a specific city.
I can compare how different eras of urbanization produced different urban forms.
I can argue for the most significant contemporary challenge facing cities globally.

categories.name: Evidence
categories.raw: Ask questions about the content, origins, purposes, and context of multiple sources in order to corroborate inferences.

competencies.raw: Ask questions about sources to corroborate inferences about urban issues.
i_can_statements: I can evaluate an urban planning document for its assumptions, priorities, and omissions.
I can compare competing accounts of the same urban development project.
I can identify whose voices are centred or marginalized in an urban planning process.

categories.name: Continuity and Change
categories.raw: Identify continuity and change in the development of urban spaces.

competencies.raw: Identify continuity and change in the development of urban spaces.
i_can_statements: I can trace how a neighbourhood changed over time through maps, photos, and data.
I can explain what forces drive urban change (gentrification, deindustrialization, immigration).
I can identify what aspects of a city's character have persisted despite change.

categories.name: Cause and Consequence
categories.raw: Determine and assess the long- and short-term causes and consequences of public policy decisions related to urban places.

competencies.raw: Assess the causes and consequences of urban policy decisions.
i_can_statements: I can trace the consequences of a specific planning decision (e.g., building a highway through a neighbourhood, or rezoning for density).
I can evaluate the intended and unintended consequences of a housing policy.
I can analyze the long-term effects of urban sprawl on environment, health, and community.

categories.name: Perspective
categories.raw: Explain and identify the forces that shape opinions and decision making on current issues related to urban studies.

competencies.raw: Explain the forces that shape opinions and decisions on urban issues.
i_can_statements: I can explain how different stakeholders (developers, renters, Indigenous nations, environmentalists) view the same development proposal.
I can analyze how political ideology shapes different approaches to housing or transit policy.
I can identify who benefits and who is harmed by a particular planning decision.

categories.name: Ethical Judgment
categories.raw: Recognize implicit and explicit ethical judgments in sources; make reasoned ethical judgments about current and past urban issues.

competencies.raw: Make reasoned ethical judgments about urban planning issues past and present.
i_can_statements: I can identify the ethical values embedded in a planning document (efficiency, equity, sustainability).
I can make an ethical argument about housing affordability as a right.
I can evaluate historical urban decisions (e.g., freeway construction through minority neighbourhoods) using ethical reasoning.

areas.name: Urbanization as a Global Phenomenon
areas.raw: Global urbanization trends and case studies; transnationalism and interconnectedness of urban centres.
topics: The global trend toward urbanization: data and projections
Megacities: Tokyo, Mumbai, Lagos, Mexico City — comparison and challenges
Transnational cities: global hubs like New York, London, Singapore
Rural-urban migration: push and pull factors globally
Urbanization in the developing world vs. the developed world

areas.name: Historic Settlement Patterns
areas.raw: Urbanization, rural-urban migration, suburbanization, gentrification; urban geography models; land use types.
topics: Ancient and medieval cities: walls, markets, and sacred spaces
Industrial urbanization: factory towns and tenements
Suburbanization: post-WWII growth and its consequences
Gentrification: causes, processes, and displacement
Urban geography models: Burgess's concentric zone, Hoyt's sector, multiple nuclei, urban realms
Land use types: residential, commercial, industrial, recreational, institutional, transportation

areas.name: Local and Regional Governance in BC
areas.raw: Cities within regional, national, and global urban systems; service sharing, funding models, elections; Federation of Canadian Municipalities; Union of BC Municipalities; relationships with Treaty First Nations, Bands, Métis Nation British Columbia.
topics: Local government in BC: municipalities, regional districts, and Metro regions
How municipalities are funded: property taxes, grants, development fees
Municipal elections: who runs, who votes, and why it matters
The Federation of Canadian Municipalities (FCM) and the Union of BC Municipalities (UBCM)
Relationships between municipalities and Treaty First Nations and Bands
Métis Nation BC and urban Indigenous governance

areas.name: Functions of Local and Regional Government
areas.raw: Infrastructure (water, waste); public housing, taxation, economic development; public safety, community organization support.
topics: Water and waste management: essential municipal services
Public transit: governance, funding, and planning
Public housing and affordable housing policy
Municipal economic development: attracting investment, revitalizing downtowns
Public safety: policing, fire, and emergency services
Parks and recreation: the public sphere and civic life

areas.name: Urban Planning and Design
areas.raw: Maximizing benefits and confronting challenges; First Peoples involvement in planning; livability, sustainability, public/private spaces, zoning.
topics: Zoning: what it is, how it works, and its social effects
New urbanism, smart growth, and transit-oriented development
Public spaces: parks, plazas, and the right to the city
Sustainable urban design: green roofs, bike lanes, urban forests
First Peoples involvement in urban planning: consultation, co-governance
Universal design and accessibility
The relationship between urbanization and sustainability

areas.name: Decision Making in Urban Planning
areas.raw: Political considerations, financial implications, public pressure, perceptions, social capital, policy constraints.
topics: How planning decisions get made: the political process
The role of developers, lobbyists, and citizens in planning
NIMBYism vs. YIMBYism: competing voices in planning debates
Social capital and its role in community planning
Financial constraints: how budget realities shape planning
Planning simulations: making trade-offs in a real context

areas.name: Contemporary Issues in Urban Studies
areas.raw: Social justice: housing, homelessness, poverty, crime, livability, affordability, food security, discrimination; global urban disparity; environmental factors.
topics: Housing affordability crisis: causes, consequences, and solutions
Homelessness: definition, causes, and responses
Urban poverty and food insecurity: the geography of need
Crime, safety, and policing in urban spaces
Environmental justice: who bears the cost of urban pollution?
Climate change and cities: flooding, heat islands, sea level rise
Global urban inequality: comparing cities in the Global North and South
